entries.intro: I'm proud to call {settlement_name} home. It's not perfect, but it's ours.
details: We built most of what you see here with our own hands. Every building has a story.
It's quiet, it's safe enough, and the people look out for each other. What more do you need?
reactions: I've thought about leaving, sure. But I always come back to the same answer.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} could use some work, if I'm being honest.
details: Half the fences need mending and the paths turn to mud the second it rains.
It's not that nobody cares. Everyone's just busy keeping their own place together.
reactions: It's home, though. For better or worse.

statCheck.pass: The real problem is drainage. The ground slopes the wrong way and nobody planned for it when they laid the paths.
statCheck.fail: I'm no builder. I just know it looks rough.

entries.intro: I like how {settlement_name} feels at dusk. Everything quiets down and the light turns golden.
details: People finish up their work and you can hear conversations drifting from open windows.
It's the kind of thing you don't notice until you've been away and come back.
reactions: Not every place has that. We're lucky.

entries.intro: I heard {other_settlement} has twice the market stalls we do. Makes you think.
details: Not that I'm jealous. More selection would be nice, though.
A bigger market means more strangers, more noise, more problems. Maybe what we've got is fine.
reactions: Grass is always greener, I suppose.

entries.intro: I wake up some mornings and just think: I'm glad I ended up in {settlement_name}.
details: It's not perfect, but the people look out for each other. That counts for a lot.
I've lived in places where you didn't know your neighbor's name after a year. This isn't that.
reactions: You don't appreciate a good home until you've had a bad one.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} could use a proper drainage system. Every heavy rain turns the square into a pond.
details: Twice this season I've had water seeping under the door. The threshold needs raising.
People keep talking about fixing it, but talk doesn't dig ditches.
reactions: I'd rather live with a fixable problem than move somewhere with unfixable ones.

statCheck.pass: A graded channel toward the east slope would handle most of it. The grade is already there: just needs digging.
statCheck.fail: I'm no engineer. I just know water shouldn't be where I sleep.

entries.intro: I wonder sometimes what people in {other_settlement} think of {settlement_name}.
details: We probably seem small and quiet to outsiders. That's not necessarily a bad reputation.
The traders who come through always seem comfortable here. That has to mean something.
reactions: We are what we are. No point dressing it up.

entries.intro: There's a smell in {settlement_name} when the wind comes right: woodsmoke and something sweet from the ovens. I never get tired of it.
details: My first morning here, that smell is what made me think I could stay.
You don't notice it after a while, but when you come back from traveling it hits you all at once.
reactions: Home is mostly a feeling. That smell is where mine starts.

entries.intro: If I ever left {settlement_name}, I think I'd miss the mornings most. The way the light comes over the ridge.
details: There's about ten minutes where everything turns gold. The rooftops, the smoke from the chimneys, the water in the troughs.
I've lived here long enough that I almost stopped noticing. Almost.
reactions: You don't realize what makes a place home until you imagine it gone.

entries.intro: Every visitor who comes through {settlement_name} says the same thing: it's smaller than they expected.
details: Then they stay a night, eat a meal, and suddenly they're asking how much a plot of land costs.
Size was never the point. We've got what we need and not much that we don't.
reactions: Let them underestimate us. We know what we've got.

entries.intro: The well in {settlement_name} has needed a new winch for months. Nobody wants to pay for it.
details: Everyone uses it, nobody wants to fund it. That's the eternal problem with shared things.
There was a meeting about it last week that lasted an hour: no decision, typical.
reactions: We'll fix it when the rope finally snaps. That's how decisions get made around here.

statCheck.pass: The real issue is the axle bearing, not the rope. Replace that and the winch would last another five years.
statCheck.fail: I'm no engineer. I just know it squeals when you turn it.

entries.intro: People from outside {settlement_name} always comment on how quiet it is. Like they've never heard silence before.
details: To us it's not quiet at all. I can hear the wind, the animals, someone hammering across the way.
I think they mean there's no crowd noise. No constant background of people doing things they'd rather not.
reactions: I suppose quiet is a luxury if you're not used to it.

entries.intro: There's a time of evening in {settlement_name} when everything just goes quiet. Right before the lamps come on.
details: The work's done, the fires are lit, and for a few minutes nobody's asking anything of anybody.
You can hear the wind through the trees and nothing else. It only lasts a moment.
reactions: That's the part they don't tell you about when they talk about living here. It's the best part.

entries.intro: Outsiders always say {settlement_name} is too quiet. Like that's a complaint.
details: They come through expecting excitement and leave disappointed. Good.
Quiet means nothing's gone wrong. I'll take that over interesting any day.
reactions: They don't understand it because they haven't earned it.

entries.intro: {settlement_name} isn't much to look at. I know that.
details: The buildings lean, the roads are mud half the year, and the well cover doesn't close right.
But every board was put up by someone I know, and every path goes somewhere I've walked a hundred times.

entries.intro: People from {other_settlement} think we don't know how things are done properly in {settlement_name}. We know, we just don't care.
details: They built their roads straighter and their roofs steeper. Good for them.
We built ours the way that works here. Different ground, different weather, different problems.
reactions: Let them think what they want. We're doing fine.

statCheck.pass: Building styles follow local materials and terrain. What works in one place fails in another: that's not ignorance, it's adaptation.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the technical reasons. I just know our way holds up.
